response:
The fare $\eta$ depends on the driving distance $\xi$ in a specific way that involves a base charge and an extra fee. If the distance $\xi$ is less than or equal to 4 kilometers then the fare $\eta$ equals 10 yuan. When the distance $\xi$ exceeds 4 kilometers the fare $\eta$ becomes 10 yuan plus 2 yuan for each kilometer over the limit. Fractions of a kilometer are always rounded up to the nearest whole number for calculation purposes. This rule creates a step function where the cost jumps at every integer distance beyond the initial 4 kilometers. The spring of the season brings many travelers to the airport for their journeys.

A passenger who paid 38 yuan implies that the total calculated distance was exactly 14 kilometers because 10 plus 2 times 14 equals 38. Since the actual travel distance was 15 kilometers the driver must have had some stopping time converted into distance. The difference between the actual 15 kilometers and the charged 14 kilometers means the effective distance was reduced by 1 kilometer due to rounding rules. This reduction happens when the total distance including stopped time is just below 15 kilometers. The city rule states that 5 minutes of stopping time equals 1 kilometer of driving distance. Therefore the maximum stopping time allowed to keep the charge at 38 yuan is 4 minutes and 59 seconds. Another spring day will see the same taxi rules applied to new passengers. The driver must manage time carefully to avoid extra charges for the customer.
